heading: SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO XLI
card_summary: This chunk of Campanini's introductory essay covers two major topics. First, it concludes his discussion of Zorzi's probable pilgrimage to the Sinai peninsula, marshaling textual evidence from the Problemata — including Zorzi's knowledge of the fifteen-day distance between Jerusalem and Sinai, the rock sheltering Moses, and a reference to pre-Torah Hebrew inscriptions along the route from Elim to Sinai that Zorzi claims to possess — and comparing these inscriptions to the Nabataean rock carvings of Wadi al-Mukatteb, linking Zorzi to a Venetian milieu of exotic-alphabet collectors including Abraham de Balmes, Giovanni Panteo, and Antonio de Fantis. Second, it opens the section on the De Harmonia Mundi itself, describing the triadic structure of the three canticles (modeled on Dante's Commedia), the eight toni per canticle corresponding to musical scale degrees, and the central theological schema: God as Artifex and Archetype in Canticle I, Christ as Wisdom, Word, and Mediator in Canticle II, and Man as the locus of cosmic harmony and microcosm-macrocosm synthesis in Canticle III.
essay:
# Chunk 8 — Saggio Introduttivo XLI (pp. 41–44)

## Part One: Closing the Sinai Dossier

Campanini concludes his sustained argument that Zorzi undertook a pilgrimage to Mount Sinai by adducing further passages from the *Problemata*. The geographical knowledge displayed is precise: Zorzi twice notes that the physical Jerusalem and Sinai "distano quindici giornate l'una dall'altro" (are fifteen days' journey apart), distinguishing carefully between the earthly topography and the spiritual "luoghi superiori che corrispondono a questi inferiori" (higher places that correspond to these lower ones). This distinction — the physical versus the anagogical Sinai-Jerusalem axis — is cited from Paul's Galatians 4:25, and Zorzi's repeated insistence on the literal distance shows, paradoxically, a grounding in empirical geography that underlies his symbolic reading.

> Non si riferisce però al monte Sinai e a Gerusalemme secondo la materia (che si trovano a quindici giornate di distanza) ma a quei luoghi superiori che corrispondono a questi inferiori.

<cite>p. 41, from the Problemata, first passage on Sinai-Jerusalem distance</cite>

### The Rock of Moses

Campanini draws attention to a passage in the *Problemata* where Zorzi describes the rock that, according to pilgrimage legend, bent to shelter Moses during the theophany. Zorzi writes that the trace of this miracle is still visible, suggesting firsthand autopsy:

> Perché fu ordinato a Mosè di stare sulla roccia, in cui fu testimone di molte visioni arcane? Forse stette sulla roccia che gli fece posto (fino ad oggi si può vederne traccia) perché potesse essere più saldo e non cadesse a causa del terrore suscitato da quell'insolita visione?

<cite>p. 42, Problemata passage on Moses and the rock</cite>

The parenthetical "fino ad oggi si può vederne traccia" is Campanini's smoking gun: it implies visual, personal inspection of the site.

### The Hebrew Alphabet Question and the Wadi al-Mukatteb Inscriptions

The most intellectually dense passage concerns Zorzi's treatment of the Hebrew alphabet. He distinguishes between the Aramaic/Chaldean square script used at the time of the Torah's giving and an earlier alphabet the Israelites employed during the Exodus. Crucially, Zorzi claims to possess physical specimens ("abbiamo presso di noi le lettere dell'alfabeto di quelle iscrizioni") of inscriptions carved by the Israelites during their journey from Elim to Sinai:

> Gli ebrei all'uscita dell'Egitto non adoperavano quella lingua o quei caratteri, ma altri. Ciò risulta con evidenza da molte iscrizioni che gli stessi ebrei scolpirono nel corso del loro viaggio da Elima al monte Sinai: abbiamo presso di noi le lettere dell'alfabeto di quelle iscrizioni. Quindi si può congetturare con una certa chiarezza che Dio, nell'atto di concedere la legge, dette anche quella lingua sacra, i cui caratteri vengono peraltro impiegati dai Caldei, che li hanno presi dagli ebrei per la loro superiore bellezza e per i misteri che contengono.

<cite>pp. 43–44, Problemata passage on Exodus inscriptions and the sacred language</cite>

Campanini identifies these specimens as almost certainly the celebrated Nabataean rock inscriptions of the **Wadi al-Mukatteb** (literally "the written valley"), a well-known pilgrimage waypoint on the route to the Monastery of St. Catherine. These inscriptions were routinely attributed in the Renaissance to the Hebrew people during the Exodus, though modern scholarship dates the bulk of them to the Nabataean culture of the 2nd–3rd centuries CE. Campanini notes that the first published transcription of a Sinaitic Nabataean inscription appeared only a century after Zorzi, in Athanasius Kircher's *Prodromus Coptus sive Aegyptiacus* (Rome 1636), based on materials collected by Fra Tomaso da Novara. Zorzi's possession of such specimens therefore places him among the earliest Western owners of such material, whether obtained in situ or through a Venetian intermediary.

### The Venetian Network of Exotic Alphabets

Campanini raises the alternative hypothesis that Zorzi need not have visited Sinai personally to obtain these specimen alphabets. He points to a rich Venetian milieu of alphabet collectors, notably **Antonio de Fantis** of Treviso, whose "biblioteca magica" may have been the source of various exotic scripts including the Enochian, Solomonic, Angelic, and the "alphabet of the river crossing" (*del passaggio del fiume*). Campanini traces the transmission of such alphabets through a cascade of early-sixteenth-century texts: Abraham de Balmes's *Peculium Abrae* (Venice 1523); Giovanni Agostino Panteo's *Voarchadumia* (1530); Geoffroy Tory's *Champ Fleury* (1529); Cornelius Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia* (1530–31); Guillaume Postel's *Linguarum duodecim characteribus differentium alphabetum* (1538); Teseo Ambrogio's *Introductio in chaldaicam linguam* (1539); down to Blaise de Vigenère's *Traicté des chiffres* (1586) and later compilations by Claude Duret and Giulio Bartolocci. Zorzi's Venetian context — shared with Panteo and De Balmes — makes the local acquisition scenario entirely plausible, even if the Sinai visit is probable on other grounds.

Campanini closes this section with a characteristically nuanced verdict: whatever the physical source of Zorzi's alphabetic specimens, the *Problemata* passages collectively reveal a thinker who reads the Holy Land simultaneously as literal geography and as living allegory — "la capacità di vedere simboli e allegorie in ogni sasso e in ogni volto della Terrasanta senza per questo privarli della loro realtà geografica ed etnografica."

---

## Part Two: Opening of Section III — The *De Harmonia Mundi*

The chunk's second movement opens a new major section of the *Saggio introduttivo* devoted to Zorzi's masterwork. The epigraph is drawn from Nicolas Le Fèvre de la Boderie — the French translator of the *De Harmonia* — with the verse fragment "Le Scerafin vole" (the Seraph flies), evoking the Isaianic seraphim and the soaring ambition of Zorzi's project.

### Title and Structural Schema

The full title — *De Harmonia mundi totius cantica tria* — is parsed by Campanini as encapsulating the scope, ambition, and architecture of the work. The triadic division into three **cantici** consciously invokes Dante's *Commedia* as a formal model, but the deeper motivation is the pervasive Trinitarian-triadic metaphysics that governs Zorzi's cosmology: all reality is structured in three dimensions — angelica, celeste, terrestre (angelic, celestial, terrestrial).

Each canticle is subdivided into **eight toni**, corresponding to the eight notes of the musical scale — a structure that makes the work's very organization an enactment of its thesis about universal harmonic proportion. The toni are further subdivided into a variable number of chapters.

### The Three Cantici: Theological Assignments

- **Canticle I** — centered on God, consistently named *Artefice* (Artificer) and *Archetipo* (Archetype). The Neoplatonic vocabulary is explicit: God as the demiurgic craftsman of the *Timaeus* and the Plotinian One.
- **Canticle II** — treats Christ, assigned the attributes of *Sapienza* (Wisdom), *Verbo* (Word/Logos), and *Mediatore* (Mediator). This Christology is deeply inflected by Kabbalistic Chokhmah and the Johannine Logos tradition.
- **Canticle III** — dedicated not, as one might expect, to the Holy Spirit, but entirely to **Man** (*l'uomo*). Campanini flags this as "sorprendentemente" surprising unless one grasps the underlying Hermetic doctrine: the human being is the *locus* where cosmic harmony becomes fully actual. The third canticle functions simultaneously as an eschatological preparation — surveying scriptural metaphors for the world to come — and as the culmination of the microcosm-macrocosm synthesis.

### The Microcosm-Macrocosm Axis

Campanini identifies the controlling philosophical framework as the integration of microcosm and macrocosm (*l'integrazione di microcosmo e macrocosmo*), through which every aspect of creation is harmonized with God, in whose image man was made. The third canticle, after reviewing mathematical proportions and musical harmonies linking body to soul "in stile platonico," moves toward an eschatological finale.

This structural description establishes the *De Harmonia Mundi* as one of the most ambitious synthetic projects of the Renaissance: a Trinitarian cosmology organized as musical composition, drawing simultaneously on biblical exegesis, Kabbalistic numerology, Neoplatonic metaphysics, and Hermetic anthropology, with Man — not the Holy Spirit — as the surprising apex of the universal harmony.
